seo.title: Surat Thani Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Complete guide to Surat Thani Province: gateway to Koh Samui, Koh Phangan, Koh Tao & Khao Sok National Park. Largest southern province at 12,891 km², 19 districts. Plan your trip.

provinceNameThai: สุราษฎร์ธานี
overview: Surat Thani is the largest province in southern Thailand, spanning 12,891 km² along the western shore of the Gulf of Thailand. Known as the 'City of Good People,' the province serves as the primary gateway to Thailand's most famous Gulf islands — Koh Samui, Koh Phangan, and Koh Tao. Beyond its islands, Surat Thani boasts Khao Sok National Park with one of the world's oldest evergreen rainforests, the stunning Ang Thong Marine National Park archipelago, and a rich culinary heritage featuring fresh Gulf seafood and southern Thai specialties. The provincial capital functions as a major transport hub connecting travelers to the islands by ferry and catamaran.
geography: Surat Thani Province covers 12,891 km², making it the largest province in southern Thailand and the sixth-largest in the country. The mainland terrain ranges from coastal lowlands along the Gulf of Thailand to mountainous rainforest in the west, where the Phuket Range forms the border with Ranong and Phang Nga provinces. The province includes over 100 islands in the Gulf of Thailand, most notably the Samui archipelago (Koh Samui, Koh Phangan, Koh Tao) and the 42-island Ang Thong archipelago. The Tapi River, the province's main waterway, flows through the capital city into Bandon Bay. Cheow Lan Lake (Ratchaprapha Dam) in Khao Sok National Park is a major freshwater reservoir surrounded by towering limestone karsts.
highlights: Koh Samui — Thailand's second-largest island with palm-fringed beaches, luxury resorts, Big Buddha temple, and Chaweng nightlife
Koh Phangan — famous worldwide for the Full Moon Party on Haad Rin Beach, plus secluded beaches and yoga retreats
Koh Tao — world-class scuba diving and snorkeling at affordable prices, with vibrant coral reefs and whale sharks
Khao Sok National Park — ancient rainforest older than the Amazon, with Cheow Lan Lake, floating bungalows, and diverse wildlife
Ang Thong Marine National Park — pristine 42-island archipelago with emerald lagoons, sea caves, and panoramic viewpoints
Chaiya Historical Park — ancient Srivijaya-era ruins and Wat Phra Borommathat Chaiya, one of Thailand's oldest surviving temples
Surat Thani Night Market — bustling local market with fresh Gulf seafood, southern Thai curries, and regional street food
Koh Nang Yuan — tiny triple-island connected by white sand bars, consistently rated among Thailand's most beautiful viewpoints

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Jan–Apr: Dry season on the Gulf coast with temperatures of 25–33°C, calm seas, and sunny skies. Ideal for island hopping, diving, and beach holidays. Highest accommodation prices on Koh Samui and Koh Phangan, especially around Full Moon Party dates.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: May–Jun & Dec: Transition months with occasional rain but generally pleasant conditions. December can still see some rainfall on the Gulf coast (different from Andaman side). Good value for accommodation with fewer crowds.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jul–Nov: Gulf of Thailand monsoon season with frequent rain and rougher seas, peaking in October–November. Ferry services may be disrupted during storms. Hotel prices drop 30–50% on the islands. Khao Sok National Park is best avoided October–November due to flooding.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit January to April for the best Gulf coast weather and calm seas for diving. Note that Surat Thani follows the Gulf monsoon pattern — the opposite of Phuket and Krabi on the Andaman coast. Budget travelers can find excellent deals in July–September when rainfall is moderate compared to the October–November peak.

topAttractions.name: Khao Sok National Park
topAttractions.description: Home to one of the world's oldest evergreen rainforests (over 160 million years old), Khao Sok features towering limestone karst formations, Cheow Lan Lake with floating bamboo raft houses, and diverse wildlife including gibbons, hornbills, and the rare Rafflesia flower. The park offers jungle trekking, kayaking, night safaris, and overnight stays on the lake surrounded by dramatic cliffs.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 300 THB (foreigners), 100 THB (children); Cheow Lan Lake: additional 300 THB

topAttractions.name: Ang Thong Marine National Park
topAttractions.description: A stunning archipelago of 42 islands in the Gulf of Thailand featuring towering limestone cliffs, hidden saltwater lagoons, sea caves, and pristine coral reefs. The highlight is the Emerald Lake (Thale Nai), an inland saltwater lake on Koh Mae Ko visible from a dramatic clifftop viewpoint. Day trips depart from Koh Samui and Koh Phangan by speedboat or catamaran.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 300 THB (foreigners), 150 THB (children); day tours from Koh Samui: 1,500–2,500 THB

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Borommathat Chaiya
topAttractions.description: One of the most important historical temples in southern Thailand, dating back to the Srivijaya period (8th–10th century). The restored chedi is a rare example of Srivijaya architecture in Thailand and is believed to contain relics of the Buddha. The adjacent Chaiya National Museum displays Srivijaya-era artifacts, bronze statues, and stone inscriptions found in the region.

topAttractions.name: Koh Nang Yuan
topAttractions.description: A tiny trio of islands connected by stunning white sand bars, located just off the northwest coast of Koh Tao. The viewpoint from the hilltop offers one of Thailand's most photographed panoramas. The surrounding waters provide excellent snorkeling with abundant coral and marine life. Accessible by longtail or speedboat from Koh Tao (15 minutes).
topAttractions.entranceFee: 100 THB landing fee; boat from Koh Tao: 100–200 THB

topAttractions.name: Big Buddha Temple (Wat Phra Yai), Koh Samui
topAttractions.description: Koh Samui's most iconic landmark features a 12-meter golden seated Buddha statue on a small island (Ko Fan) connected to the northeast coast by a causeway. The temple complex includes ornate dragon stairways, guardian statues, and smaller shrines. The hilltop location offers views across the Gulf of Thailand. A busy temple market sells souvenirs, food, and local crafts at the base.

topAttractions.name: Cheow Lan Lake (Ratchaprapha Dam)
topAttractions.description: A vast man-made reservoir within Khao Sok National Park, surrounded by dramatic limestone karsts rising hundreds of meters from the emerald-green water. Often compared to Ha Long Bay in Vietnam, the lake offers overnight stays in floating raft houses, kayaking through narrow gorges, and swimming in crystal-clear waters. Wildlife spotting opportunities include gibbons, langurs, and diverse bird species in the surrounding rainforest.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 300 THB (included in Khao Sok park fee); longtail boat tours: 1,500–3,000 THB

sources.sourceName: My Thailand Tours — Cheow Lan Lake Fees

topAttractions.name: Surat Thani Night Market (Talat San Chao)
topAttractions.description: The bustling night market near the Tapi River in downtown Surat Thani is the best place to experience authentic southern Thai street food. Stalls serve fresh Gulf oysters (Surat Thani is famous for its giant oysters from Kanchanadit), grilled seafood, khanom chin with spicy fish curry, and local desserts. The market operates nightly and is popular with locals rather than tourists, offering genuine prices.

localFood: Hoi Nang Rom Sot (Fresh Oysters) — Surat Thani's GI-certified giant oysters from Kanchanadit, among the largest in the world, served raw with lime, garlic, and chili or charcoal-grilled with dipping sauce
Khanom Chin Tai (Southern Rice Noodles) — fermented rice noodles served with intensely spicy southern fish curry, pickled vegetables, and fresh herbs; the quintessential southern Thai breakfast
Kaeng Pu (Crab Curry) — rich coconut curry with fresh local crab meat, betel leaves, and southern spice paste; a coastal Surat Thani specialty
Khao Yam — spicy rice salad tossed with toasted coconut, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, dried shrimp, and budu (fermented fish sauce); eaten as a light meal or snack
Pad Sataw Kung (Stink Bean Stir-Fry) — pungent petai beans stir-fried with shrimp, shrimp paste, and chili; a southern Thai signature with a bold, distinctive flavor
Khai Khem Chaiya (Chaiya Salted Eggs) — salt-cured duck eggs from the Chaiya district, used in curries, fried rice, and eaten on their own; a registered local product
Tom Khlong Pla (Sour Smoked Fish Soup) — smoky, sour soup made with locally smoked fish, tamarind, shallots, and lemongrass; a Surat Thani comfort dish
culture: Surat Thani's name means 'City of Good People,' bestowed by King Vajiravudh (Rama VI) in 1915. The province has deep historical roots as a center of the ancient Srivijaya maritime empire (8th–13th century), evidenced by the ruins at Chaiya which may have served as the empire's capital. Today, the province is predominantly Buddhist Thai with a significant Muslim minority, especially in coastal fishing communities. The annual Chak Phra Festival (October) is Surat Thani's signature event, featuring illuminated boat processions on the Tapi River and elaborate float parades carrying Buddha images — coinciding with the end of Buddhist Lent. The Rambutan Fair (August) celebrates the province's status as Thailand's largest rambutan producer with parades, fruit competitions, and cultural performances.
gettingThere: Surat Thani Airport (URT) receives direct flights from Bangkok (1h 15min, from 1,000–3,500 THB one-way with AirAsia, Thai Lion Air, Nok Air). Koh Samui Airport (USM) has direct flights from Bangkok, Chiang Mai, Singapore, and Hong Kong (operated by Bangkok Airways, from 2,500–8,000 THB). Overnight trains from Bangkok's Hua Lamphong station take 9–12 hours (sleeper berth: 500–1,400 THB). VIP buses from Bangkok's Southern Terminal take 9–10 hours (500–900 THB). From Surat Thani town, ferries to Koh Samui depart from Donsak Pier (1.5h, 250–400 THB) and Tapee Pier (night ferry, 6h, 200 THB). High-speed catamarans (Lomprayah, Seatran) connect to Koh Phangan (2.5h) and Koh Tao (3.5h).
gettingAround: Songthaew (shared pickup trucks) and local buses connect Surat Thani town with surrounding districts for 30–80 THB. Grab is available in Surat Thani city for car rides (40–300 THB). On Koh Samui, songthaew run fixed routes along the ring road (40–100 THB), and motorbike rental is popular (200–300 THB/day). Koh Phangan and Koh Tao are best explored by motorbike (150–250 THB/day). Car rental on Koh Samui starts from 800 THB/day. Inter-island ferries and speedboats connect Koh Samui, Koh Phangan, and Koh Tao multiple times daily (200–600 THB per crossing). For Khao Sok, most visitors arrange transport through their accommodation or join organized tours from Surat Thani.
travelTips: Ferry schedules to the islands change seasonally — always book through Lomprayah or Seatran Discovery official sites to avoid scams at the bus station
Surat Thani town is primarily a transit hub — most travelers spend no more than one night before heading to the islands or Khao Sok
Koh Samui Airport is privately owned by Bangkok Airways, making flights expensive — save money by flying to Surat Thani (URT) and taking a ferry-bus combo
Full Moon Party on Koh Phangan draws 10,000–30,000 people — book accommodation weeks in advance and secure return ferry tickets before the party
Gulf coast monsoon season (Oct–Nov) is opposite to the Andaman coast — if Phuket/Krabi are rainy, Surat Thani islands may still have decent weather, and vice versa
Khao Sok floating bungalows on Cheow Lan Lake require advance booking — walk-ins are rarely possible, especially December to April
ATMs on Koh Tao charge 220 THB per withdrawal — withdraw larger amounts on Koh Samui or mainland Surat Thani to minimize fees
Night trains from Bangkok to Surat Thani are a budget-friendly classic — book upper berth sleepers for the best value (around 600 THB)
